title: Backup restore-test evidence — RPO/RTO SLO compliance per recovery tier
exclusions: Does NOT evaluate the integrity of the restored data content (UC-22.45.2 — immutability and tamper detection) nor does it audit the existence of a backup at all (UC-22.45.3 — coverage gap). This UC operates strictly on the population of workloads that DO have a backup AND DID have a restore test executed within the look-back window. It also does not evaluate application-level recoverability after the data is restored (database consistency, application bring-up) — that is a tier-1/tier-2 BCP-rehearsal concern owned by UC-22.45.5. Lab-only or pre-production environments are out of scope: filter by `regulated_class!="non-prod"` if your lookup tags them.
evidence: Weekly saved search `UC-22.45.1` produces a per-target × tier finding row whenever the measured RPO or RTO exceeded its SLO, or the restore test itself failed (status != pass/success/ok). The output is split into three streams: (a) ServiceNow GRC `RESILIENCE-RPO-RTO-BREACH` ticket per finding routed to the named `business_owner`, (b) signed CSV in `index=audit_evidence` (7y retention, RFC3161 countersignature) for the regulator binder, (c) weekly executive PDF showing tier-1 success rate, mean measured RPO vs. SLO, mean measured RTO vs. SLO over the trailing 13 weeks. The positive attestation (everything inside SLO) is summary-indexed to `audit_evidence` as well so the auditor can sample either side.

compliance.assurance_rationale: CP-9 (System Backup) explicitly requires that organisations 'conduct backups of [system / user-level / system documentation] information at [organisation-defined frequency]' AND CP-9(1) requires testing of backup information to verify media reliability and information integrity. Continuous measurement of RPO/RTO against a declared SLO directly evidences both the frequency and the testing-validation legs of the control — full assurance.
compliance.controlObjective: Continuously evidences CP-9 and CP-9(1) by measuring actual restore-test RPO and RTO against the tier-defined SLO every week and emitting a finding when either is breached. Paired with UC-22.45.3 and UC-22.45.5 for the full CP-9 evidence package.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search `UC-22.45.1` daily output in `index=audit_evidence`. Each row carries measured RPO/RTO, per-tier SLO, test_id, timestamp, business owner. Bundled into the quarterly CP-9 evidence pack with RFC3161 countersignature. 7-year retention.

compliance.assurance_rationale: DORA Art.12(2) explicitly requires financial entities to 'establish backup systems that can be activated in accordance with the backup policies and procedures, including recovery time and recovery point objectives'. RPO and RTO are named in the regulatory text — measuring them weekly against a published SLO IS the evidence the supervisor will sample for during a TLPT or thematic review.
compliance.controlObjective: Demonstrates to the DORA competent authority that backup-system activation is regularly tested and that the measured RPO/RTO match the documented objectives in the ICT BCP. Sampling the saved-search output answers the supervisor's evidence request without manual reconstruction.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search `UC-22.45.1` digest in `index=audit_evidence` (5+ year retention satisfies Art.27). Paired with UC-22.45.3 and UC-22.45.5 for the joint Art.12 + Art.24 evidence package.

compliance.assurance_rationale: A1.2 — 'The entity authorises, designs, develops or acquires, implements, operates, approves, maintains and monitors environmental protections, software, data backup processes, and recovery infrastructure to meet its objectives'. Continuously measured RPO/RTO restore tests are exactly the monitoring evidence a Type II SOC 2 attestation requires across the audit period.
compliance.controlObjective: Provides the SOC 2 service auditor with a continuous-monitoring record (rather than an annual checkbox) of backup recovery effectiveness; one Saved search firing weekly across the 6 / 12 / 18-month audit window produces N samples instead of N=1.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search `UC-22.45.1`. Auditor receives the full attestation feed in `audit_evidence` plus the dashboard PDFs over the SOC 2 reporting period.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Inject a synthetic restore-test event for `target=SAP-PRD-01`, `tier=1`, `rpo_seconds=21600` (6h), `rto_seconds=18000` (5h), `test_result=pass`. With `dr_tiers.csv` declaring tier-1 SLOs of `rpo_slo_seconds=14400` (4h) and `rto_slo_seconds=14400` (4h), the search MUST emit a row with `finding=rpo_and_rto_breach`, `measured_rpo_s=21600`, `rpo_slo_seconds=14400`, `measured_rto_s=18000`, `rto_slo_seconds=14400`. Inject a second event for `target=WEB-PRD-01`, `tier=2`, `test_result=fail` — must emit `finding=restore_failed` regardless of measured RPO/RTO.
controlTest.negativeScenario: A successful restore test for `target=DB-PRD-01`, `tier=1`, `rpo_seconds=10800` (3h), `rto_seconds=7200` (2h), `test_result=pass` against the same tier-1 SLOs MUST NOT appear in the output. A tier where `dr_tiers.csv` does not declare an SLO (i.e. `rpo_slo_seconds=null`) MUST NOT appear — those workloads are filtered out at the `where isnotnull(rpo_slo_seconds)` step, and the gap is itself surfaced by a sister-search ('untiered workloads in restore-test stream').

dataSources: Primary: `index=backup sourcetype=backup:restore_test` ingested via the Splunk Add-on for Veeam Backup & Replication (Splunkbase 5601), which polls Veeam Enterprise Manager `/api/restore-points/states` and `/api/sureBackupJobs/states` and writes one event per completed SureBackup job. Supplementary HEC ingest path for Commvault Command Center (`/v4/jobs?jobFilter=Restore`), Veritas NetBackup OpsCenter (`/opscenter/v2/jobs?jobType=Restore`), Rubrik Atlas CDM (`/api/v2/event?event_type=Restore`), Cohesity Helios DataProtect (`/v2/data-protect/runs?runTypes=kRestore`), and Dell PowerProtect Data Manager (`/api/v3/activities?category=PROTECTION_RESTORE`). Cloud sources: AWS Backup restore jobs via the Splunk Add-on for AWS (Splunkbase 1876) CloudWatch Events on `aws.backup`, and Azure Backup restore jobs via the Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Cloud Services (Splunkbase 3110) Recovery Services Vault diagnostic category `AzureBackupReport`. Required fields per event: `target_system | vm_name | client_name | host` (any one — the SPL coalesces), `test_result | status | job_status` (one of pass/success/ok or fail), `rpo_seconds | recovery_point_objective_seconds`, `rto_seconds | recovery_time_objective_seconds`, `tier` (1/2/3 — aliased from the backup-engine job tag or joined from the CMDB at parse time), and `test_id` (vendor job identifier). Lookup: `dr_tiers.csv` (tier → `rpo_slo_seconds`, `rto_slo_seconds`, `tier_name`, `business_owner`) maintained by the Resilience team, refreshed quarterly when DR tiering is reviewed by the steering committee. Evidence archive index: `index=audit_evidence` (7y, signed).
app: Splunk Add-on for Veeam Backup & Replication (Splunkbase 5601), Splunk Add-on for AWS (Splunkbase 1876), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Cloud Services (Splunkbase 3110). Optional: Splunk Enterprise Security (Splunkbase 263) for notable-event escalation when a tier-1 system breaches; Splunk IT Service Intelligence (Splunkbase 1841) for service-tree linkage so a restore-test failure surfaces against the affected business service.
spl:
index=backup sourcetype=backup:restore_test earliest=-7d@d latest=@d
| eval target=coalesce(target_system, vm_name, client_name, host)
| eval test_status=lower(coalesce(test_result, status, job_status))
| eval rpo_s=coalesce(rpo_seconds, recovery_point_objective_seconds)
| eval rto_s=coalesce(rto_seconds, recovery_time_objective_seconds)
| stats max(rpo_s) as measured_rpo_s,
        max(rto_s) as measured_rto_s,
        latest(test_status) as test_status,
        latest(test_id) as test_id,
        latest(_time) as last_test_at
        by target tier
| lookup dr_tiers.csv tier OUTPUT rpo_slo_seconds rto_slo_seconds business_owner tier_name
| where isnotnull(rpo_slo_seconds)
| eval rpo_breach=if(measured_rpo_s > rpo_slo_seconds, 1, 0)
| eval rto_breach=if(measured_rto_s > rto_slo_seconds, 1, 0)
| eval restore_failed=if(NOT match(test_status,"pass|success|ok"), 1, 0)
| where rpo_breach=1 OR rto_breach=1 OR restore_failed=1
| eval finding=case(restore_failed=1, "restore_failed",
                    rpo_breach=1 AND rto_breach=1, "rpo_and_rto_breach",
                    rpo_breach=1, "rpo_breach",
                    rto_breach=1, "rto_breach")
| eval last_test_at=strftime(last_test_at, "%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S%z")
| table tier tier_name target finding measured_rpo_s rpo_slo_seconds measured_rto_s rto_slo_seconds test_id last_test_at business_owner
| sort 0 tier - measured_rto_s
description: Aggregates restore-test events by `target` × `tier` over the trailing 7 days, joins each row to the tier's declared RPO/RTO SLOs via `dr_tiers.csv`, and emits a finding whenever (a) the measured RPO exceeded the SLO, (b) the measured RTO exceeded the SLO, or (c) the test itself failed. The `finding` field is mutually exclusive with priority `restore_failed > rpo_and_rto_breach > rpo_breach > rto_breach` so each row carries one operational classification. The 7-day window is intentionally 1× the typical tier-3 testing cadence — workloads that haven't been tested at all in the window also fall out (no rows produced), and are caught by the companion 'no-test-in-cadence' sister-search documented in Step 3.
value: Replaces the 'we tested DR last year, here are the screenshots' artefact with a continuous, sortable, signed evidence record. RPO/RTO breaches normally surface to the IT Operations team during a real disaster — the worst possible time to find out. With this UC, a breach surfaces during the weekly test, while the only thing at stake is a Tuesday-night ticket, not a board call. Tied to the GRC ticketing system, each breach has an owner, an age, and an SLA, so 'the business doesn't know about it' stops being a defence. For the regulator, the saved search output IS the testing-effectiveness evidence — there is no second artefact to construct.
implementation: (1) Stand up the restore-test data feed for every backup engine in scope — Veeam SureBackup is the gold path; Commvault, NetBackup, Rubrik, Cohesity, PowerProtect, AWS Backup, Azure Backup follow via HEC or vendor TA. (2) Maintain `dr_tiers.csv` as a quarterly-reviewed lookup with the documented SLOs from the BIA; never let the SLO drift from policy without a steering-committee decision. (3) Schedule the search weekly Monday 06:00 site-local (after weekend tests complete, before the Monday standup). (4) Wire to ServiceNow GRC `RESILIENCE-RPO-RTO-BREACH`; require the business owner to acknowledge within the tier's RTO. (5) Summary-index findings AND positive attestations to `audit_evidence`; RFC3161-countersign the weekly digest. Indexes: `backup`, `audit_evidence`. Sourcetypes: `backup:restore_test` (canonical), `aws:backup:restore`, `azure:recovery:restore` (per-cloud variants normalised at parse time). Lookups: `dr_tiers.csv`.
visualization: Dashboard `UC-22.45.1 — Restore-test RPO/RTO`: (1) Trailing-13-week pass/breach heatmap of `tier × week`, colour-stopped at 100%/95%/<95%. (2) Single-value 'tier-1 systems within SLO this week' with sparkline. (3) Sortable findings table from the SPL with row drilldown to `index=backup sourcetype=backup:restore_test target=$row.target$ earliest=-90d` to show the restore-test history of one workload. (4) Box plot of `measured_rto_s` per tier overlaid with the SLO horizontal line — surfaces tiers where the typical test is creeping close to the SLO before any breach occurs. (5) Per-business-owner row count, ageing buckets (0-3d / 3-7d / >7d) — used in the weekly Monday standup. (6) Trend line of mean RPO and mean RTO over 26 weeks, separated by tier, against the SLO — answers 'is our recovery posture stable, drifting better, or drifting worse?' for the steering pack.

references.title: Splunk Add-on for Veeam Backup & Replication (Splunkbase 5601)
Veeam Enterprise Manager REST API — SureBackup
AWS Backup — Events for backup, copy, and restore jobs
Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Cloud Services (Splunkbase 3110)
Azure Backup — AzureBackupReport diagnostic category schema
NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5 — CP-9 System Backup
AICPA Trust Services Criteria (2017, Rev. 2022 PoF) — A1.2

knownFalsePositives:
**(1) Maintenance windows on the backup proxy that intentionally extend restore time.** Quarterly proxy upgrades, ESXi host moves, or storage vMotion of the restore-target datastore can produce a one-off RTO breach that is operationally legitimate. Suppress via a `maintenance_windows.csv` lookup (`start_at`, `end_at`, `affected_targets`, `change_ref`) and append `| lookup maintenance_windows.csv target OUTPUT change_ref start_at end_at | where isnull(change_ref) OR last_test_at < strptime(start_at,"%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S%z") OR last_test_at > strptime(end_at,"%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S%z")` before the breach evaluation. The CAB-approved change reference is the auditable trail.

**(2) Test-environment SureBackup verifications running against scaled-down resources.** A Veeam SureBackup virtual lab is intentionally CPU/RAM-constrained — the boot/restore time it measures is NOT the production RTO. Tag those job groups `is_lab=1` in the Veeam configuration, propagate via Veeam's job-description metadata, and append `| where coalesce(is_lab,0)=0` to the production search. Lab-RTO is still valuable for trend analysis — surface in a separate dashboard panel labelled clearly.

**(3) Application-consistent vs crash-consistent restore types reporting different RTO.** A crash-consistent restore is faster but excludes the application-quiesce step required by the BIA; the difference can be 15-60 minutes for a database. Distinguish via a `restore_type` field at ingest (Veeam emits this; AWS Backup tags the job; Commvault reports it). The production search should filter `restore_type IN ("application-consistent","transaction-consistent")` so crash-consistent restores don't artificially flatter the metric.

**(4) Stale `dr_tiers.csv` after a BIA refresh.** When the steering committee tightens a tier-1 RTO from 4h to 2h, every system that's been testing 'green' against the old SLO suddenly turns red. This is correct behaviour — the SLO change re-baselines the population — but it produces a one-time spike that needs operational explanation. Communicate ahead of every BIA cycle; pin the previous SLO row alongside the new one with `effective_from` / `effective_until` columns and use the historical row for previous-period reporting.

**(5) Job-name renames between TA versions.** Veeam 12 introduced the `Backup` → `BackupJob` rename in some API responses; the TA started reporting `vm_name` differently. After every TA upgrade, run the field-extraction check in Step 3 BEFORE letting the next scheduled run alert. False positives during a 24-hour TA-rollback window are common and confusing.

**Suppression mechanism for explicit exceptions:** Maintain `restore_test_exception_register` KV Store with (`target`, `granted_by`, `valid_from`, `valid_until`, `reason`, `compensating_control`). Open-ended exceptions are forbidden — `valid_until` is required. The SPL appends `| lookup restore_test_exception_register target OUTPUT valid_until reason | where isnull(valid_until) OR valid_until < now()` and surfaces expired exceptions as a sister-search alert so the register can't be allowed to atrophy.
equipmentModels: AWS Backup (CloudWatch Events source `aws.backup`, detail-type `Restore Job State Change`)
Azure Backup (Recovery Services Vault diagnostic category `AzureBackupReport`)
Cohesity DataProtect (Helios cluster v7.x — REST API /v2/data-protect/runs?runTypes=kRestore)
Commvault Backup & Recovery 11.x (Command Center REST API /v4/jobs?jobFilter=Restore)
ServiceNow GRC IRM (Vancouver / Washington / Xanadu) for ticketing
Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Cloud Services (Splunkbase 3110)
Splunk Add-on for Veeam Backup & Replication (Splunkbase 5601)
Splunk Enterprise Security 7.x (Splunkbase 263) for notable-event escalation
Splunk IT Service Intelligence 4.x (Splunkbase 1841) for service-tree overlay
Veeam Backup & Replication 12.x (Enterprise Manager REST API /api/sureBackupJobs/states)

premiumApps.name: Splunk Enterprise Security
premiumApps.note: optional; tier-1 breach raises an ES notable for the resilience analyst

premiumApps.name: Splunk ITSI
premiumApps.note: optional; provides workload → business-service mapping in the steering pack

detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

- **Splunk:** Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (Victoria). Restore-test events are small (~2 KB each) and infrequent (one per restore-test job completion, typically ≤1k events/week even at large enterprises) — the search is cheap. Place it on the regulatory-reporting search head if you have one, otherwise any general-purpose SH.
- **Indexes:** `backup` (raw restore-test events, 90d hot retention is sufficient since the search only looks back 7 days), `audit_evidence` (7y, signed; the regulator binder lives here). Set `frozenTimePeriodInSecs = 220924800` (7 years) on `audit_evidence`.
- **Add-ons (mandatory if the engine is in scope):**
  - Veeam — `Splunk Add-on for Veeam Backup & Replication` (Splunkbase 5601, current 2.x).
  - AWS Backup — `Splunk Add-on for AWS` (Splunkbase 1876) with the `aws_inputs` CloudWatch Logs subscription on `/aws/events/aws.backup` (or EventBridge → CloudWatch Logs) configured in every region in scope.
  - Azure Backup — `Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Cloud Services` (Splunkbase 3110) with diagnostic settings on every Recovery Services Vault forwarding the `AzureBackupReport` category.
- **Add-ons (optional, depends on engines in scope):** Commvault TA (Splunkbase 7115), Rubrik TA (Splunkbase 4587), Cohesity TA (Splunkbase 6043), Dell PowerProtect TA (Splunkbase 6800). If the vendor TA does not exist for your version, use the HEC fallback documented in Step 1b.
- **Lookup `dr_tiers.csv`:** must be deployed to the search-tier app. Required columns:
  - `tier` (key — `1` / `2` / `3` / `4`, string).
  - `tier_name` (human-readable: `Mission-critical`, `Business-critical`, `Standard`, `Best-effort`).
  - `rpo_slo_seconds` (e.g. tier-1 = 14400 / 4h; tier-2 = 43200 / 12h; tier-3 = 86400 / 24h).
  - `rto_slo_seconds` (e.g. tier-1 = 14400 / 4h; tier-2 = 28800 / 8h; tier-3 = 86400 / 24h).
  - `business_owner` (named role with email, e.g. `head-of-it-ops@example.com` — NOT a personal email).
  - `effective_from` (ISO-8601 date the row became authoritative — used to keep the historical SLO when the steering committee retunes).
  - `effective_until` (nullable; closes off a tier definition when retuned).
- **RBAC:** the saved search runs as `svc_compliance_uc_22_45_1`. It needs `srchIndexesAllowed = backup,aws,azure,audit_evidence` and read on the lookup. The ServiceNow alert action needs the ServiceNow modular alert action installed on the SH.
- **Time-zone discipline:** every TA must EVAL `_time` from the vendor's reported completion timestamp at index time. If `_time` is the indexing time, `last_test_at` will drift by the ingest delay (sometimes hours) and the SLO arithmetic is wrong. Verify with `index=backup sourcetype=backup:restore_test | eval ingest_delay=index_time-_time | stats avg(ingest_delay) p99(ingest_delay) by sourcetype` — ingest delay should be under 60 seconds.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Four engines plus two cloud-native paths plus the HEC fallback covers the typical estate. Walk through each one in scope.

### 1a — Veeam SureBackup (gold path)

Splunk Add-on for Veeam Backup & Replication ships with a `veeam_sure_backup_state` input. Configure on the heavy forwarder closest to the Veeam Enterprise Manager (REST traffic is bursty during a SureBackup run):
```ini
[veeam_sure_backup_state://surebackup]
veeam_em_host = veeam-em.example.com
veeam_em_port = 9398
veeam_em_user = svc_splunk_readonly
veeam_em_password_secret = svc_splunk_readonly_pwd
resource = /api/sureBackupJobs/states
interval = 600
index = backup
sourcetype = backup:restore_test
```
For SureBackup itself to produce restore-time and recovery-point metrics, configure the SureBackup job in Veeam Backup & Replication Console → Home → SureBackup → New Job. Critical settings:
- **Virtual lab:** isolated, sized within 30% of the production VM's CPU/RAM (lower violates FP suppression #2 above).
- **Application group:** include every VM the auditor expects to see tested — at minimum, one representative VM per tier per business service.
- **Linked job:** the production backup job (so SureBackup verifies the latest restore-point).
- **Test types:** enable 'Heartbeat', 'Ping', and 'Application' tests. The 'Application' test is the one that produces a meaningful RTO measurement; without it the metric reflects only boot time.
- **Schedule:** weekly on Sunday 02:00. SureBackup completion times for a 50-VM application group typically run 30-60 minutes.
SureBackup writes the recovery-point timestamp (= `_time of the linked production backup`) and the restore-completion timestamp; the TA computes `rpo_seconds = NOW - recovery_point_timestamp` and `rto_seconds = restore_completion_timestamp - sure_backup_start_timestamp`. Confirm the TA version emits these fields with `index=backup sourcetype=backup:restore_test | fieldsummary | search field IN (rpo_seconds,rto_seconds)`.

### 1b — Commvault / NetBackup / Rubrik / Cohesity / PowerProtect (HEC fallback)

If a vendor TA is unavailable for your version, run a small Python collector on the backup admin's automation host that polls the vendor restore-test API on a schedule and POSTs to HEC. The canonical schema is:
```json
{
  "event": "restore_test_completed",
  "sourcetype": "backup:restore_test",
  "index": "backup",
  "_time": "2026-05-12T03:11:38+00:00",
  "target_system": "SAP-PRD-01",
  "tier": "1",
  "test_id": "<vendor-job-id>",
  "test_result": "pass",
  "restore_type": "application-consistent",
  "rpo_seconds": 21384,
  "rto_seconds": 6420,
  "recovery_point_at": "2026-05-11T21:15:00+00:00",
  "restore_completed_at": "2026-05-12T03:11:38+00:00",
  "vendor": "commvault|netbackup|rubrik|cohesity|powerprotect"
}
```
The collector must compute `rpo_seconds` and `rto_seconds` server-side (do NOT delegate the maths to Splunk — vendors disagree on which field is the canonical restore start, and the conversion is brittle if every SH has to re-derive it). Reference vendor endpoints:
- Commvault Command Center 11.x: `GET /webconsole/api/v4/jobs?jobFilter=Restore&completedFrom=<epoch>` returns `jobId`, `startTime`, `endTime`, `clientName`, `subclientName`. Compute `rto_seconds = endTime - startTime`.
- NetBackup OpsCenter 10.x: `GET /opscenter/v2/jobs?jobType=Restore&jobState=Completed&modifiedAfter=<iso>` returns `jobId`, `startTime`, `endTime`, `clientName`, `restorePointInTime`. Compute `rpo_seconds = endTime - restorePointInTime`.
- Rubrik Atlas CDM 8.0+: `POST /api/v2/event?event_type=Restore` with body filter `objectName`, `eventStatus=Success|Failure`. Restore-test events include `recoveryTimeObjectiveSeconds` and `recoveryPointTimestamp` natively.
- Cohesity Helios 7.x: `GET /v2/data-protect/runs?runTypes=kRestore&startTimeUsecs=<microsec>`. Field `permitRestoreObject.rpoUsecs` ÷ 1e6 = `rpo_seconds`.
- Dell PowerProtect Data Manager 19.x: `GET /api/v3/activities?category=PROTECTION_RESTORE`. Field `result.status` ∈ {OK, ERROR}; `endTime - startTime` = `rto_seconds`; `restorePoint.copyTime` for `rpo_seconds`.

### 1c — AWS Backup

AWS Backup emits CloudWatch Events whenever a restore job changes state. Subscribe via the Splunk Add-on for AWS:
```ini
[aws_cloudwatch_logs://aws_backup_jobs]
aws_account = svc_splunk_readonly
region = us-east-1
log_group_name = /aws/events/aws.backup
index = aws
sourcetype = aws:cloudwatch_logs:backup
```
In `props.conf` on the SH:
```
[aws:cloudwatch_logs:backup]
TRANSFORMS-clone_to_restore_test = aws_backup_restore_clone

[aws_backup_restore_clone]
REGEX = "detail-type":"Restore Job State Change"
FORMAT = sourcetype::backup:restore_test

[backup:restore_test]
EVAL-target_system = coalesce('detail.resourceArn', 'detail.metadata.LogicalResourceId')
EVAL-test_result = case('detail.state'=="COMPLETED","pass", 'detail.state'=="FAILED","fail", true(),lower('detail.state'))
EVAL-rto_seconds = strptime('detail.completionDate','%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S%z') - strptime('detail.startTime','%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S%z')
EVAL-rpo_seconds = strptime('detail.completionDate','%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S%z') - strptime('detail.recoveryPointCreationDate','%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S%z')
EVAL-tier = coalesce('detail.metadata.Tier', '1')
```
Do this in every region in scope — AWS Backup is regional, and a missing region is a silent coverage gap.

### 1d — Azure Backup

On every Recovery Services Vault: Diagnostic settings → Add → Categories: `AzureBackupReport` → Send to Event Hub. Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Cloud Services consumes the Event Hub. Map at parse time:
```
[azure:recovery:diagnostic]
TRANSFORMS-clone_to_restore_test = azure_restore_clone

[azure_restore_clone]
REGEX = "category":"AzureBackupReport".+"OperationName":"Restore Job"
FORMAT = sourcetype::backup:restore_test

[backup:restore_test]
EVAL-target_system = 'properties.BackupItemUniqueId'
EVAL-test_result = case('properties.JobStatus'=="Completed","pass", 'properties.JobStatus'=="Failed","fail", true(),"unknown")
EVAL-rto_seconds = 'properties.JobDurationInSecs'
EVAL-rpo_seconds = strptime('properties.JobOperationSubType','%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S%z') - strptime('properties.RecoveryPointTime','%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S%z')
```

### 1e — `dr_tiers.csv` lookup

The minimum viable lookup that produces correct output:
```csv
tier,tier_name,rpo_slo_seconds,rto_slo_seconds,business_owner,effective_from,effective_until
1,Mission-critical,14400,14400,head-of-it-ops@example.com,2026-01-01,
2,Business-critical,43200,28800,head-of-applications@example.com,2026-01-01,
3,Standard,86400,86400,it-service-mgr@example.com,2026-01-01,
4,Best-effort,604800,604800,it-asset-mgr@example.com,2026-01-01,
```
Version-control this in the security-content git repo and deploy via Splunk Cloud Workload Manager or the on-prem cluster bundle. Refresh review cadence: quarterly (with the BIA review) at minimum; immediately after every BIA refresh.

## Step 2 — Implement the search and alert

Walk through the SPL:

1. `index=backup sourcetype=backup:restore_test earliest=-7d@d latest=@d` — 7-day window snapped to the day boundary so consecutive weekly runs cover disjoint periods (no double-counting, no missed events at the seam).
2. `eval target=coalesce(target_system, vm_name, client_name, host)` — every vendor names the workload differently; the canonical `target` is the first non-null. ORDER MATTERS: `target_system` is the explicit field most TAs emit; `vm_name` is the Veeam fallback; `client_name` is the Commvault/NetBackup fallback; `host` is the last-ditch fallback.
3. `eval test_status=lower(coalesce(test_result, status, job_status))` — every vendor's pass/fail field is named differently; normalise to lowercase string.
4. `eval rpo_s=...` and `eval rto_s=...` — same coalesce pattern.
5. `stats max(rpo_s) as measured_rpo_s, max(rto_s) as measured_rto_s ... by target tier` — collapse multiple tests of the same target in the window to the WORST (max) RPO and RTO. Reasoning: if a workload was tested twice and one test was bad, we want to see the bad one. The latest test by `_time` is captured separately via `latest(_time)` so the runbook can pivot.
6. `lookup dr_tiers.csv tier OUTPUT rpo_slo_seconds rto_slo_seconds business_owner tier_name` — quarterly-reviewed SLOs.
7. `where isnotnull(rpo_slo_seconds)` — exclude untiered workloads (they're a finding for the sister-search, not this one).
8. Three boolean evals + the breach filter — keeps only the rows that fail at least one criterion.
9. `eval finding=case(...)` — the mutually-exclusive classification.
10. `eval last_test_at=strftime(...)` — ISO-8601 string for the auditor.
11. `table ... | sort 0 tier - measured_rto_s` — most-overdue tier-1 RTO breach at the top.

### Schedule (`savedsearches.conf`)
```ini
[UC-22.45.1: Restore-test RPO/RTO breach]
disabled = 0
enableSched = 1
cron_schedule = 0 6 * * 1
dispatch.earliest_time = -7d@d
dispatch.latest_time = @d
alert.suppress = 1
alert.suppress.period = 167h
alert.suppress.fields = target,finding
action.summary_index = 1
action.summary_index._name = audit_evidence
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.45.1,reg=NIST_800-53,reg=DORA,reg=SOC2
action.servicenow_grc = 1
action.servicenow_grc.category = RESILIENCE-RPO-RTO-BREACH
action.servicenow_grc.assigned_to_field = business_owner
action.email = 1
action.email.to = resilience-leadership@example.com
action.email.subject = UC-22.45.1: $result.target$ tier-$result.tier$ $result.finding$
```
Weekly Monday 06:00 cadence catches the weekend's SureBackup runs and gives the resilience team the standup hour to triage before the Monday business day in the affected region begins.

### Optional ES notable for tier-1 breaches
```ini
action.correlationsearch.enabled = 1
action.correlationsearch.label = UC-22.45.1 — Tier-1 restore-test breach
action.notable = 1
action.notable.param.rule_title = Tier-1 restore-test breach: $target$ ($finding$)
action.notable.param.rule_description = Tier-1 system $target$ failed restore-test SLO ($finding$); measured RPO $measured_rpo_s$s vs SLO $rpo_slo_seconds$s, RTO $measured_rto_s$s vs SLO $rto_slo_seconds$s.
action.notable.param.security_domain = audit
action.notable.param.severity = high
```
Attach as a post-process filter `| where tier=="1"`.

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

### (a) Cross-check Veeam SureBackup against Veeam UI

1. In Veeam Backup & Replication Console → Home → Last 24 hours, pick a completed SureBackup run.
2. Note the start time, duration, and result.
3. In Splunk:
   ```spl
   index=backup sourcetype=backup:restore_test test_id="<that-job-id>" earliest=-1d
   | table _time, target_system, test_result, rpo_seconds, rto_seconds, test_id
   ```
4. The row's `_time` must match the Veeam UI completion timestamp to within 60 seconds; `test_result` must match the UI's reported result; `rto_seconds` must match the UI's duration to within 5 seconds.
5. If `rpo_seconds` looks much larger than the linked job's age, the TA is computing it against the wrong restore-point timestamp. Recheck the TA version and the `EVAL-rpo_seconds` mapping.

### (b) Cross-check AWS Backup against the AWS console

1. AWS Console → AWS Backup → Restore jobs → pick a completed restore.
2. Note `Restore job ID`, `Completion date`, `Status`.
3. In Splunk:
   ```spl
   index=backup sourcetype=backup:restore_test test_id="<restore-job-id>" earliest=-1d
   ```
4. Row should be present; `target_system` should match the AWS Backup resource ARN suffix.
5. If absent: check the AWS TA input (region match), check the CloudWatch Events rule actually fires (AWS Console → CloudWatch → Events → Rules), check the props/transforms clone-to-`backup:restore_test` regex matches the actual event payload.

### (c) Lookup freshness and shape
```spl
| inputlookup dr_tiers.csv
| stats count by tier
```
Every tier in the lookup should be represented; row count change >20% between consecutive runs warrants a sanity check on the upstream BIA export.

### (d) Fixture replay

Load `sample-data/uc-22.45.1-fixture.json` which contains six synthetic events: 1× tier-1 PASS within SLO (must NOT alert), 1× tier-1 RPO breach, 1× tier-1 RTO breach, 1× tier-1 both, 1× tier-2 FAIL, 1× untiered (no `tier` in lookup — must NOT alert). Run the search; expect exactly 4 rows. The fixture also includes the dashboard panels' expected screenshots in `sample-data/expected/UC-22.45.1-dashboard/*.png` for manual visual comparison.

### (e) Negative test — search returns zero on a clean week

Disable the breach-injection mechanism (or use a known-clean fixture) and re-run. Expect zero rows; expect an automated check that the summary index gets a 'zero-result attestation' record stamped so the regulator can see the search WAS run, it just had no findings.

### (f) Companion 'no-test-in-cadence' sister-search

Workloads that ARE backed up but never have a restore test won't appear in this UC at all (they don't produce events in `backup:restore_test`). Run weekly:
```spl
| inputlookup regulated_workloads.csv
| where isnull(decommissioned_at)
| join type=left workload_id [ search index=backup sourcetype=backup:restore_test earliest=-90d | stats max(_time) as last_test_at by target | rename target as workload_id ]
| eval days_since_test = round((now()-last_test_at)/86400,1)
| where isnull(last_test_at) OR days_since_test > coalesce(test_cadence_days, case(tier=="1",7, tier=="2",30, tier=="3",90, true(),90))
| table regulated_class workload_id tier days_since_test cmdb_ci business_owner
```
This closes the silent-gap loophole and is itself referenced by CP-9(1) (testing).

## Step 4 — Operationalise

### Dashboard `UC-22.45.1 — Restore-test RPO/RTO`

Panel-by-panel:
1. **Row 1 — tier tiles.** Four single-value panels (one per tier) showing 'this week within SLO': count of unique `target` that PASSED ÷ count of unique `target` that ran a test. Stoplight thresholds 100% / 95% / <95%. Drilldown opens this UC's findings table filtered to the tier.
2. **Row 2 — finding table** straight from the production SPL. Row drilldown opens a per-target history view filtered to the last 90 days of `backup:restore_test` events for that target.
3. **Row 3 — 13-week trend.** Line chart of mean `measured_rto_s` per tier vs the tier's `rto_slo_seconds` (horizontal reference line). Catches creeping degradation before it crosses the threshold.
4. **Row 4 — owner ageing.** Table grouped by `business_owner` × age bucket (0-3d / 3-7d / >7d / >14d). Used as the input to the Monday standup.
5. **Row 5 — restore-test cadence overlay (from sister-search).** Single-value showing 'regulated workloads with no restore test in cadence' counted per tier.

### Runbook per finding type

**On `finding=restore_failed`:**
1. Pull the test_id; open the vendor UI to the job; review the engine's reported error. Most common causes: virtual lab boot-script timeout (Veeam), source-snapshot expiration (AWS Backup), credential-store rotation (Commvault).
2. Re-run the restore-test on the next eligible recovery-point; if it fails twice in a row, treat as an active outage of the recovery capability — engage the vendor and the resilience-on-call.
3. Confirm the workload's primary backup IS succeeding (sister-search `index=backup sourcetype=backup:job_status target_system="<target>" status=success earliest=-7d`). A failed restore-test against a successful backup is recoverable; a failed restore-test against a chain of failing backups is a P1 incident.

**On `finding=rpo_breach`:**
1. Question 1: when did the workload's backup cadence last change? `index=changes object_type=backup_job target="<target>" earliest=-30d` should surface a CAB record if the schedule was deliberately relaxed.
2. If not: the backup is failing silently between successes. Pull the engine's job-history.
3. If the schedule WAS relaxed and the new cadence violates the SLO, the BIA needs to be re-opened — escalate to the steering committee, not handled in the operational queue.

**On `finding=rto_breach`:**
1. Compare to peers: `index=backup sourcetype=backup:restore_test tier="<tier>" earliest=-30d | stats avg(rto_seconds) p95(rto_seconds) by tier` — if every tier-1 workload is slow, the bottleneck is shared (backup-network bandwidth, restore-engine capacity, lab capacity). If only one workload is slow, the bottleneck is specific (over-sized VM, missing array-snapshot integration).
2. If shared, escalate to capacity-planning with the measured numbers. If specific, file a vendor case OR a workload-architecture review.

**On `finding=rpo_and_rto_breach`:**
This is a P1 from a regulatory perspective. The workload is both too-old-to-restore AND too-slow-to-restore. Engage the resilience-on-call immediately, page the workload's business owner, and log the breach in the GRC system with an explicit 'continuity-risk-high' flag.

### Weekly evidence pack

A scheduled report runs every Monday 08:00 site-local, exports the dashboard to PDF via the Splunk reports framework, summary-indexes the full findings table plus the positive-attestation table (no findings = positive attestation stamped explicitly) to `audit_evidence`, and RFC3161-countersigns the PDF + CSV bundle. Path convention: `evidence-packs/cp-9/restore-test-rpo-rto/<year>/<isoweek>/UC-22.45.1.{pdf,csv,tsr,sig}`.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

### Vendor-specific failure modes

- **Veeam SureBackup virtual lab CPU/RAM ratio.** If the lab is too small relative to production, the RTO measurement is inflated and you'll get persistent tier-1 breaches that disappear after a lab resize. Document the lab sizing in the SureBackup job description, alert on `restore_type='lab' AND lab_resource_ratio < 0.7` (where the ratio is computed from a sister-search on the Veeam lab metadata API).
- **Veeam Backup & Replication 12.x changed the `SureBackupSession` JSON path.** TA versions before 2.1.0 misparse the embedded RTO; upgrade to 2.1.0+ before relying on the measurement. Detect with `index=backup sourcetype=backup:restore_test | fieldsummary | search field=rto_seconds | where count<<expected`.
- **Commvault Command Center reports `endTime` as UTC but `startTime` in the client's local timezone (DST-affected) on releases before 11.36.** Yields negative `rto_seconds` for restores that cross a DST boundary. Either upgrade Commvault or normalise both timestamps at the collector with the client's `gmtOffset`.
- **NetBackup `OpsCenter` REST API rate-limits to 100 jobs per request.** The collector script must paginate with the `nextPageToken` and back off on HTTP 429. Without pagination, the collector silently drops the older runs in a busy hour.
- **Rubrik Atlas marks restores triggered from snapshots older than the SLA-domain retention as `Warning` not `Success`.** This is a Rubrik product quirk — the restore actually succeeded. Either add `Warning` to the pass set OR fix the SLA domain in Rubrik. The pass set is currently `pass|success|ok`; consider adding `warning` if your environment relies on this behaviour.
- **Cohesity Helios `kRestore` runs include synthetic-full operations that are NOT user-initiated restore tests.** Add a `where runType!=kSyntheticFull` filter at the collector level.
- **PowerProtect Data Manager activity payload lacks the workload-tier metadata** — populate `tier` at the collector using a lookup against the PPDM `protectionPolicy` → `serviceLevelObjective` → tier mapping cached locally.
- **AWS Backup restore from a cross-region copy** reports a `recoveryPointCreationDate` in the SOURCE region's timezone metadata. Use the field `creationDate` (UTC) for `rpo_seconds`, not `recoveryPointCreationDate` — the latter is the user-visible date and can mislead the RPO calc.
- **Azure Backup `JobDurationInSecs` excludes pre/post-job hooks.** If your application-consistent restore depends on a SQL pre-script that takes 20 minutes, that 20 minutes is NOT counted in the RTO. Either disable the hooks for restore-tests OR run a wrapping pipeline that times the full restore including hooks and posts to HEC as the source of truth.

### NIST 800-53 caveats

- **FIPS 199 categorisation discipline.** A High-baseline system tested against tier-2 SLOs is a baseline mismatch — flag and re-tier. Join with the system-categorisation register and alert on `fips_categorization != aligned_tier(tier)`.
- **Control inheritance from a CSP.** If AWS Backup is the protection for a workload, the CSP-controlled portion of CP-9 is inherited; the customer-controlled portion is the RPO/RTO measurement and the SLO setting. Make the inheritance assertion visible in the evidence pack.

### DORA caveats

- **Threat-led penetration tests (TLPT) under Art.26 can include backup-restore exercise.** Ensure the Saved Search excludes the TLPT-time window OR explicitly includes it with a tag — the TLPT scope owner will tell you which.
- **Article 27 record retention is 5 years.** The 7-year `audit_evidence` covers this; do NOT trim the retention to save storage without a board decision and the supervisor's awareness.

### SOC 2 caveats

- **Type II audit period typically 6 / 9 / 12 months.** Make sure your SOC 2 ToR explicitly references the saved-search output (and the index it lives in) so the auditor doesn't have to ask. The first time you sit a SOC 2 audit using this UC, walk the auditor through one week's evidence step-by-step so they understand the data model and accept it as continuous evidence rather than asking for the old quarterly screenshots.
grandmaExplanation: We test every week whether your important systems can really be restored from backup, and we measure how long it takes. If something runs slower than the agreed limit, you find out the same week — not in the middle of the next disaster, and not in front of the auditor.
